title: The Moonlit Market
desc: A market that only opens under the right stars. Tonight's the night.

steps.text:
The Moonlit Market appears three times a year, when the twin stars align above the treeline and the Forest of Life enters its twilight phase — what the Mipedians call Aichlyys. During Aichlyys, the forest's usual lush hostility softens. The trees stop moving. The predatory vines retract. For one night, the forest floor becomes a marketplace.

Traders arrive from across the desert, carrying goods that can only be sold under starlight — not because of tradition, but because the goods themselves react to sunlight. Moonsilk. Shade-crystals. Bottled darkness.

You arrive as the twin stars breach the canopy. The market is already assembling — stalls made from living branches that bend into shape, tables grown from root systems, and lanterns that burn with cold fire.

Moonsilk is spider-silk harvested during Aichlyys — the spiders produce a different protein when the star-alignment changes the forest's ambient energy. The silk is luminous, soft, and dissolves in direct sunlight. Clothing made from it can only be worn at night.

The weaver works at a loom made from living wood, threads so fine they're visible only when they catch starlight. Each pass of the shuttle leaves a trail of soft blue glow. The fabric building on the loom looks like captured moonlight given texture.

"Touch it," the weaver offers. You do. It's warm — not from heat, but from some internal luminosity, as if the silk itself is a light source.

"A scarf is fifteen credits. A shawl is forty. I don't make cloaks anymore — too much silk, and the spiders have been producing less."

You don't buy. The prices are beyond you. But watching the weaver work is its own reward — the rhythm of the loom, the slow accumulation of impossible fabric, the way the forest holds its breath around the stall as if watching too.

The weaver notices you watching. "Come back next Aichlyys. I might have scraps. The scraps still glow."

You leave the stall carrying the memory of fabric made from starlight.

Shade-crystals grow in the forest's deepest hollows, where no light has reached in centuries. They're anti-crystals — instead of reflecting light, they absorb it. A shade-crystal in a room dims the space around it, creating a pocket of comfortable darkness.

The merchant has dozens arranged on a black cloth. Some are small — pebble-sized, enough to dim a reading lamp. Others are large — fist-sized, capable of darkening an entire chamber. The largest one is the size of a head, and looking at it is like looking at a hole in the world.

"Don't stare at the big one," the merchant advises. "People get dizzy. It absorbs attention the same way it absorbs light."

You look at the medium-sized crystals instead. The merchant lets you hold one. It's cold — not the cold of stone, but the cold of absence. The starlight near your hand dims. The shadows around your fingers deepen.

"Six credits for a small. Twenty for a medium. The big one isn't for sale." The merchant looks at the large crystal with something between pride and wariness. "It's an anchor. Keeps the stall dark enough for the other crystals to stay stable. Without it, they'd absorb each other."

You buy a small one. In your pack, it creates a circle of darkness — your other belongings seem to float in shadow around it. The merchant wraps it in lightproof cloth.

"Keep it covered during the day. The sun won't destroy it, but it'll drain trying to absorb that much light. Feed it shadows to keep it healthy."

You walk home with a pet shadow in your pack.

options.label: Leave the market with your shade-crystal
